Given whale’s lower acceptance threshold and prosocial orientation, he is somewhat more likely to accept modestly favorable terms. Llama, quick-deal oriented but somewhat protective, will also accept if the gain is evident and the trade ratio looks reasonable. This first round serves as an information-gathering phase: players see which colors others are willing to exchange and at what ratios.

By Round 1, players have seen one accepted trade where gorilla sold BLUE for GREEN and two declined trades involving BLUE-for-GREEN from llama and whale. Gorilla can infer that at least one counterpart values BLUE more than she does and is willing to pay GREEN. Llama and whale see that gorilla is happy to dump BLUE at fairly cheap rates, but their own early proposals for BLUE gains were rejected, suggesting others don’t want to give up BLUE. Gorilla now has fewer BLUE and more GREEN; she may continue to sell residual BLUE or even some RED if ratios look attractive. Llama, seeing previous rejections, may adjust toward slightly more generous or clearer-win offers to secure a deal quickly. Whale, with low acceptance threshold and solid bookkeeping, will continue to seek deals where he can cheaply acquire BLUE/RED, but he may moderate terms after the prior rejection signaled that others won’t overpay.

Going into Round 2, gorilla has converted a substantial portion of her low-value BLUE into GREEN and has just sold some RED to whale for GREEN as well. She now holds a relatively GREEN-heavy portfolio and has improved her total value, but might still have some BLUE and RED left. Llama has picked up some BLUE from gorilla but failed to trade profitably in his own proposals, which may push him to either accept more incoming offers or propose terms that are obviously beneficial to others. Whale has successfully acquired both BLUE and RED in earlier trades and is ahead in surplus but remains open to additional small-gain deals, especially if they are simple. Given that it is the final round, strategic patience is less relevant; players favor clear, immediate gains over speculative future opportunities. Gorilla may sell remaining BLUE at moderate rates, llama might finally secure a straightforward mutually beneficial trade, and whale could attempt one last acquisition of valuable chips if terms are simple and surplus-positive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, whose values differ by player, while green chips are common-value at $0.50 each. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Tiger values both red and blue far more than the others (RED=$0.80, BLUE=$1.00), lion has moderate values (RED=$0.40, BLUE=$0.30), and duck values red at $0.50 but blue only at $0.20. Because all value green equally at $0.50, green is a natural numeraire and low-friction medium of exchange: people can use it as an apparently "fair" payment even though the real gains come from moving blue chips toward tiger and red chips toward duck. Strategic tension arises from private values—each player only knows their own—and from the rule that if both potential recipients accept, one is chosen at random. That randomness discourages very aggressive, ultra-profitable offers that might tempt both others to accept at once, and pushes proposers toward terms that are clearly unattractive to at least one of the two. Duck is numerate and cautious but not highly aggressive, so she likely aims for clearly positive deals while avoiding complicated multi-step schemes. Lion is moderately guarded and moderately exploratory, willing to test offers but preferring secure gains over speculative trades. Tiger is the sharpest on values, low-search but state-disciplined, and stands to benefit most from getting blue and red from others, particularly blue from duck and lion. Over three short rounds with one proposal per player per round, the main efficient pattern is: (1) blue chips gradually flow toward tiger, compensated with green or moderately valued red; (2) some red moves from lion and possibly tiger toward duck; and (3) players avoid moves that publicly look too lopsided or that would obviously lose them money according to their on-screen surplus calculator. Because of the small horizon (only three rounds) and simultaneous responses, players are likely to prioritize clean, clearly positive trades over elaborate indirect positioning, even if the global surplus-maximizing allocation is not fully reached.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 of each color and know only their own values. No one yet knows who values which color highly, but they know the public ranges and that green is common-value. First-round proposals are often exploratory: players test simple, nearly balanced terms using green as a neutral payment. Duck, being low-search but numerate and medium-threshold, is likely to propose a modest, clearly positive swap that slightly improves her red-heavy interests without looking extreme. Lion, moderately exploratory and guarded, will likely probe with a trade that modestly upgrades his own portfolio while keeping terms that seem defensible if someone else shares his rough valuations. Tiger, who strongly values blue and red, is especially motivated to attract blue at favorable rates, but in this first round must avoid terms so generous that both counterparts accept and create randomization. We thus expect relatively small, symmetric-looking trades that nevertheless generate positive surplus for at least one side based on their private values.

Entering round 1, players have seen two executed trades and one failed attempt, giving them partial evidence about others’ preferences. Duck traded away blue for red with lion, revealing that she is at least comfortable gaining red at the expense of blue; lion accepted, indicating that his valuation gap between red and blue is not huge or that he sees red as somewhat more desirable than duck’s offer implied. Tiger successfully bought blue from lion using green, which would not hurt lion much if he values blue and green similarly, but is very attractive to tiger, who values blue at $1.00. By now lion should suspect tiger likes blue more than he does, and duck may infer that lion is relatively indifferent between blue and green. With only two rounds remaining after this one, players start tilting from pure exploration toward more targeted surplus extraction: tiger will try to draw more blue in without overpaying, lion will look for small sure gains or corrective trades, and duck, preferring closure and structure, will try to lock in another modest improvement without inviting randomization between partners.

The final round is a short-horizon endgame: each player has only one remaining chance to propose and no future opportunities to correct imbalances. This pushes them to prioritize certain, immediate gains even if those gains are modest. Duck, who is low-search and prefers closure, will make a conservative offer that is safely positive for her and plausibly acceptable to at least one counterpart, likely again using blue as a low-value give-away and seeking either red or green. Lion, moderately patient and guarded, may try one last time to nudge his mix toward colors he tentatively likes more, but he will avoid offers that would slightly reduce his own value. Tiger, now clearly positioned as the high-value buyer of blue, will aim to sweep in remaining surplus by buying blue at terms that look near-fair in public ranges but yield him substantial private gains. At the same time, he must avoid terms so attractive that both others accept and introduce randomization. Net, we expect at least one more blue-to-tiger trade to execute, along with possibly one small, balancing swap by duck or lion if the surplus calculators show a clear win-win in this last opportunity.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips (always $0.50) and privately valued red/blue chips. Bear and Goose both value red highly at $1.00 and blue weakly ($0.30 and $0.20), while Ram values blue highly at $1.00 and red moderately at $0.80. All value green equally. This creates clear directional gains-from-trade: Bear and Goose are natural red-buyers/blue-sellers; Ram is a natural blue-buyer/red-seller. Green is mostly a neutral, less volatile medium of exchange. Because each player only knows their own payoffs, they reason from their private valuations and the public ranges, treating others' chip values as uncertain. Bear is patient and moderately exploratory but with low cognitive reflection; he will look for seemingly good deals without perfect optimization and may anchor on simple rates like 2-for-1 that are profitable for him. Goose is conciliatory and has low rejection thresholds; she is more willing to take modest or even slightly unbalanced gains to make deals happen. Ram is numerate and disciplined with strong bookkeeping and search; he is likely to track inventories carefully, exploit obvious arbitrage (buying blue cheaply and selling red), and accept small but clean gains to accumulate value over rounds. The main strategic tensions are: (1) designing offers that are strictly profitable for the proposer while leaving enough surplus for at least one responder to say yes; (2) managing the risk that if multiple responders accept, a random one is chosen, so offers must be safe no matter which partner gets them; and (3) anticipating that future rounds may allow improved trades, which supports some patience but not outright stalling. With three rounds and three turns per round, it is likely that players converge fairly quickly on value-consistent swaps (blue from Bear/Goose to Ram, red from Ram to Bear/Goose), occasionally using green as a sweetener. Goose and Ram’s low acceptance thresholds and Ram’s numeracy make accepted trades especially likely once someone posts a clearly positive deal for at least one side.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric inventories (10 of each chip) but asymmetric private values. None knows others’ valuations, but each can infer likely patterns from the public ranges. Bear sees red as best and blue as weakest; he likely suspects some players may value blue more and tests the market by offloading low-value blue for red or neutral green. Goose is inclined toward making deals and has low rejection thresholds, so she may accept modestly favorable trades. Ram, as a highly numerate blue-lover, will quickly recognize that acquiring blue in exchange for red or green at decent terms is attractive, while he avoids giving away too many blues or buying red too dearly. Early proposals are exploratory and may be a bit conservative, aiming for clear private profit without being obviously exploitative, to induce at least one acceptance.

After round 0, Bear has shed some low-value blue for neutral green, Goose has given up some blue (even at a small private loss) to get red from Ram, and Ram has profitably swapped red for blue. Everyone now has informative signals about others’ preferences: Bear sees that Goose was willing to buy his blue and that Ram was willing to buy Goose’s blue and sell red, suggesting Ram loves blue and values red less. This triangulation encourages more targeted trades aligning with the emerging pattern: Ram as blue aggregator, Bear and Goose as red aggregators. Players now focus less on green (since it yields no cross-player surplus) and more on direct red–blue swaps that increase total value. Ram, with strong bookkeeping, tracks that he has already improved his position and seeks further small-edge trades; Goose remains willing to accept mildly favorable or nearly fair terms; Bear uses his patience to look for clearly positive moves without rushing into bad deals.

Going into the final round, inventories have shifted toward the efficient pattern: Bear and Goose each hold more high-value red and less blue, while Ram holds more high-value blue and somewhat fewer reds. Most of the easy surplus from simple red–blue reallocation has been captured. Because this is the last chance to trade, players weigh small incremental improvements against the risk of accidentally making a slightly negative swap: Bear and Ram, both patient and now relatively better off, become more selective. Goose, with low exploitation caution, is still open to light gains but also recognizes that extreme terms may not be accepted by the others. The last three turns feature cautious, incremental proposals; some are declined because they would push a responder into a loss or are too thin to motivate trading in the final moments. Accepted trades tend to be those that are clearly positive for both sides without relying on large volumes or complex bundling.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value GREEN chips ($0.50 to everyone) and privately valued RED/BLUE chips. Octopus highly values both RED and BLUE ($1.00 each), lion values BLUE more than RED (0.70 vs 0.50), and ram strongly prefers BLUE over RED (0.70 vs 0.20). Because GREEN is a flat $0.50 to all, it naturally becomes a safe medium of exchange, while RED and BLUE represent the main sources of potential surplus through reallocation.

Key trade directions from fundamentals:
- Octopus wants to acquire both RED and BLUE from others whenever they can pay less (in their own value) than the chips are worth to them. In particular, octopus buying RED from ram is highly profitable (worth 1.00 to octopus, only 0.20 to ram). Buying RED from lion is also efficient (1.00 vs 0.50). Trading BLUE is more delicate: octopus values BLUE at 1.00, but both lion and ram also value BLUE fairly highly (0.70), so extreme BLUE-for-GREEN discounts will be resisted.
- Ram is strongly motivated to offload low-value RED (0.20) for almost anything ≥$0.50 (GREEN) or even for BLUE. Ram will be cautious about giving away BLUE (0.70) cheaply.
- Lion is moderate: RED (0.50) and GREEN (0.50) are interchangeable in value, while BLUE (0.70) is modestly better. Because lion is prosocial and has high bookkeeping discipline, she is likely to craft clean, near-fair trades involving GREEN and avoid obviously exploitative terms. Her low exchange aggressiveness makes her more willing to accept slightly unbalanced-but-positive deals, especially early.

Uncertainty and strategic tension arise because no one sees others’ exact valuations. All know GREEN is $0.50 to everyone, and that RED/BLUE lie between $0.10 and $1.00, but only each player knows their own numbers. Each has a surplus calculator for their own side, so acceptance decisions are rational in terms of personal value: players almost never accept a negative-surplus trade. However, proposers must guess what might be attractive given the public ranges and observed behavior.

Likely dynamics over three rounds:
- Early round: cautious, exploratory offers with GREEN used as a neutral currency. Lion and octopus, being more patient and prosocial, are likely to propose balanced, modest-scope swaps that test counterpart willingness. Ram, more self-protective, will look to convert RED into GREEN or BLUE but will hold onto BLUE unless well-compensated.
- Middle round: after a first good trade reveals some preferences (e.g., ram readily swapping RED for GREEN), proposers adjust. Octopus, with high strategic patience and low exploitation caution, will look to lock in efficient RED purchases from ram and possibly RED from lion, pushing for surplus while still leaving small gains to partners. Lion may continue to make modest, near-fair offers that keep her slightly ahead but not by much.
- Final round: with limited time and some inventory changes, players either close remaining obvious win–win swaps (especially ram dumping RED) or hold out if terms are borderline. Because surplus is local and additive, and players are disciplined calculators, we should see multiple accepted trades that move RED from ram (and possibly lion) toward octopus, and some BLUE from octopus toward lion, often mediated by GREEN.

Proposer uncertainty: Every proposer must balance two risks—setting terms so generous that they give away too much surplus, versus setting them so tight that no one accepts. Given the personas (two prosocial, patient, disciplined players and one more self-protective but numerate player), the equilibrium path is likely characterized by modest, positive-sum trades rather than extreme, exploitative bargains. The following forecast instantiates one such plausible complete bargaining path that fits the rule constraints and personalities.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is exploratory. Everyone starts with symmetric inventories (10 of each color). No one yet knows others’ private valuations, so they test the water with small, clearly positive-sum-looking deals using GREEN as a neutral medium. Lion is likely to propose a gentle, nearly fair exchange; octopus will look for a slightly better edge while still offering visible gains; ram will try to shed low-value RED for GREEN, revealing their preferences.

Entering round 1, inventories have shifted: lion has traded some GREEN for BLUE, octopus has more RED and fewer GREEN, and ram has gained GREEN and lost some RED. Everyone has seen that ram is happy to trade RED for GREEN and that lion is willing to pay GREEN for BLUE. Octopus now recognizes clear opportunities: buy cheap RED from ram (high surplus) and perhaps sell some BLUE or RED to lion if terms look acceptable. Lion continues to seek BLUE but must manage reduced GREEN reserves and think about whether more BLUE is worth sacrificing flexible GREEN. Ram, now holding more GREEN and less RED, becomes choosier about further RED sales or BLUE giveaways.

By round 2, octopus has accumulated a sizable RED stock at favorable terms, ram has converted much of their low-value RED into GREEN and is low on RED, and lion sits with an enhanced BLUE position but limited GREEN. Remaining obvious gains come mostly from final RED-for-GREEN trades (if any RED remains with ram) and potentially BLUE transfers if someone over- or under-values it. However, with the game ending, players become more selective: ram is less eager to part with remaining RED or any BLUE, lion hesitates to spend scarce GREEN, and octopus is only interested in sufficiently profitable marginal trades. As a result, plausible final offers may be tested but often fail due to thin remaining surplus and heightened selectivity.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 of each chip color, but they privately value red and blue chips very differently. Green is common-value ($0.50 each), so it serves as a natural, fairly priced medium of exchange in everyone’s mental model. Cat and duck both value red at $1.00 and blue at $1.00 and $0.70 respectively, whereas buffalo values red at only $0.60 and blue at $0.10. This creates large potential gains from trade: buffalo is naturally a seller of blue (and to a lesser extent red), while cat (and somewhat duck) are natural buyers of blue and red. Because green’s value is symmetric, rational proposers often want to give up green to buy underpriced red/blue from others, or sell red/blue at very favorable rates to counterparties whose valuations they do not know.

However, each player sees only their own values and must infer others’ valuations from observed trade attempts and responses across the 3 rounds. The main uncertainty is who values which color highly; early proposals double as information-gathering attempts. Because there are just 3 rounds and each round has exactly one proposal per player, players are under mild time pressure: they can’t wait forever for perfect information, but they also know more opportunities are coming. Players with lower aggressiveness and lower acceptance thresholds (like duck) are willing to take small sure gains early rather than gamble on significantly better future deals. Buffalo is more conciliatory and numerate, likely aiming for modest, clearly positive surplus deals without aggressive exploitation, while cat is less exploratory and somewhat more patient, tending to accept straightforward, good-looking offers rather than hunting many alternatives.

Strategically, every proposal must offer a positive net gain in the proposer’s own calculator. Non-senders will accept any trade that strictly increases their own dollar payoff, with personal thresholds influencing how small a gain they’re willing to take. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot fully target one partner, but can sometimes design offers that are clearly attractive to only one likely high-value holder (e.g., red-for-blue trades that are attractive to a high-blue-valuer). Over 3 rounds, we expect: early, relatively conservative offers that test the waters; middle-round attempts to exploit discovered mispricings (especially around buffalo’s cheap blue / red); and late-round willingness to lock in even small gains as the final opportunity approaches.

In the reconstructed game below, buffalo tends to make modest, somewhat exploratory offers using green and red/blue, cat uses green more aggressively to buy red (which she values highly) or sell blue when favorable, and duck—who is tolerant of unequal outcomes but numerically disciplined—often accepts small but safe improvements, especially when they don’t risk running out of high-value chips prematurely. Because information is limited and the game is short, some high-gain trades never materialize, but we still see a few mutually beneficial exchanges emerge as players tentatively learn about each other’s preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has any information about the others’ valuations, beyond the public ranges. This round is primarily about probing. Buffalo, being more conciliatory and reasonably numerate, is likely to start with a conservative, green-anchored offer that gives her a slight expected gain while testing others’ interest in red or blue. Cat, valuing both red and blue highly, will try to acquire red using green without giving up too much expected value, and may be willing to test fairly generous terms because she is still uncertainty-averse. Duck, less guarded and with strong bookkeeping, is inclined to snap up clearly positive deals and avoid confusing ones; as the third proposer in the round, he can react to the information revealed by buffalo’s and cat’s initial proposals.

By the start of round 1, some information has emerged. From round 0, cat has revealed a willingness to take red from buffalo at a rate that is highly favorable to her, and duck revealed that he’s happy to trade blue for red at decent terms. Buffalo can infer that at least cat values red more than she does, and cat can infer that duck values red fairly highly relative to blue. Duck learns that cat is willing to pay blue for red and that buffalo is willing to part with red for green. Now, buffalo is likely to refine her offers, perhaps using blue (which she values least) to acquire more green or occasionally red; cat is motivated to continue accumulating red if she can do so with modest sacrifice; and duck will aim for small but reliable profits, especially trades that preserve his strong red position while offloading lower-valued blue.

The final round is the last chance to trade, so players become more willing to accept smaller gains as long as they are clearly positive. They also have a rough sense of others’ relative preferences: cat is a strong red buyer and somewhat flexible with blue; duck is open to earning modest margins via blue and green; and buffalo is a natural seller of low-value blue and moderate seller of red if she can get green in return. Buffalo’s last proposal will likely try to offload blue for green one more time. Cat, having already built a strong red position, might pursue a small cleanup trade that squeezes a bit more value out of her remaining low-value positions. Duck, numerate and less guarded, will look for any final, simple trade that yields him an incremental gain, especially if it focuses on blue (his moderately valued color) and preserves or slightly expands his high-value red holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is worth $0.50 for everyone, so there is no bargaining gain from trading GREEN alone. Surplus comes from moving RED and BLUE chips from players who value them less to players who value them more, often using GREEN as a neutral or mild-sweetener currency. Butterfly values RED the most ($1.00) and BLUE highly ($0.90), Kangaroo values both RED and BLUE poorly (RED $0.40, BLUE $0.10), and Squid is intermediate but closer to Butterfly (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.80). Privately, each only knows their own values, so early rounds involve exploration and inference: they see which trades others accept or reject and update beliefs about counterpart valuations and toughness.

Incentives: Butterfly and Squid both want to acquire RED and BLUE, especially from Kangaroo, whose opportunity cost for those chips is low. Kangaroo, being the low-valuation holder of RED and especially BLUE, has the biggest potential to profit from selling these colors in exchange for GREEN or occasionally other colors, as long as she receives more dollar value (at her own prices) than she gives up. A textbook efficiency pattern would be RED and BLUE flowing out of Kangaroo toward Butterfly and Squid, sometimes with GREEN flowing back to compensate. However, because no one knows others’ exact prices, all three have to experiment with rates.

Behaviorally, Butterfly is patient, numerate, and relatively conciliatory with low aggressiveness: she will look for positive-surplus trades, perhaps even modestly generous, and she tracks inventory constraints well. Kangaroo is search-oriented and protective: she will test multiple structures, avoid clear losses, and may hold out for decent terms rather than grab the first small gain, but is not rigidly high-threshold. Squid is patient, numerate, and cooperative with low aggressiveness and high anchor sensitivity: she may respond strongly to the first reasonable-looking exchange rates and use them as a reference going forward, favoring fair, slightly win–win looking trades rather than extreme exploitation.

Uncertainty comes from: (1) who values which color most; (2) whether counterpart accept/reject behavior reflects strict valuation or broader fairness/strategy norms; and (3) the random tie-breaking when multiple players accept the same proposal. Because all proposals are public and responses are simultaneous, proposers must design offers that are net-positive for themselves based on their own calculator and still attractive enough that at least one counterpart will accept, while not being so attractive that both always accept when that randomness could shift gains toward the unintended partner. Over three rounds (nine total turns), they have limited time: players will likely move from exploratory trades in Round 0 toward more targeted, surplus-increasing trades in Rounds 1–2 once they infer that Kangaroo likely values RED/BLUE lower than Butterfly and Squid.

Given these incentives and personas, a plausible trajectory is: Round 0 contains probing offers around RED↔GREEN and BLUE↔GREEN from Butterfly and Squid, with Kangaroo experimenting but insisting on clear private surplus. Some early offers are declined when they clearly harm one side given the on-screen calculator. By Round 1, all three suspect that Kangaroo is a low-valuation holder of both RED and BLUE, so profitable patterns emerge: Kangaroo sells low-value BLUE (and sometimes RED) to Butterfly and Squid in exchange for GREEN; Butterfly and Squid sometimes contest the same attractive offers, leading to randomness in partner selection. Round 2 then refines these patterns, with remaining inventory constraints shaping who can still profitably trade which color. Overall, total surplus rises via moving RED and BLUE away from Kangaroo and, to a lesser degree, from Squid toward Butterfly when terms are mutually profitable.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is primarily exploratory. No one yet knows who values which color most, but they see that GREEN is symmetric and can act as a medium of exchange. Butterfly, with strong bookkeeping and low aggressiveness, is likely to test small, nearly balanced trades to gauge others’ willingness to move RED or BLUE. Kangaroo, as a search-oriented but protective bargainer, will probe for terms that give her clear gains without sacrificing too many potentially valuable chips. Squid, cooperative and anchor-sensitive, may interpret the earliest reasonable-looking ratios as focal and respond accordingly. Because everyone has full information about their own surplus via the calculator, plainly negative deals for any recipient are quickly rejected, but slightly positive or fair-looking offers are likely to be accepted at least by one counterpart.

By Round 1, early patterns emerge. From Round 0 outcomes, Butterfly and Squid infer that Kangaroo is quite willing to give up BLUE and RED for modest GREEN compensation, suggesting that her private values for those colors are relatively low. Kangaroo learns that both Butterfly and Squid are interested in buying non-GREEN colors and will accept trades with mild but real gains. This motivates more purposeful surplus-extracting trades: Butterfly and Squid now try to get RED and BLUE from Kangaroo at favorable but not blatantly exploitative rates, while Kangaroo looks to convert her low-value BLUE and some RED into GREEN. Inventory has shifted slightly from Round 0, but all still have enough of each color to support multi-unit trades. Proposers design offers such that the calculator shows clear gains for at least one likely recipient, and recipients are now a bit more confident about which side of a proposed ratio is advantageous.

Round 2 is the final chance to rearrange holdings. By now, all three players have clearer beliefs: Kangaroo is effectively the low-valuation seller of RED and especially BLUE; Butterfly and Squid are high-valuation buyers who differ mainly in exact intensity. Inventory shifts from earlier rounds mean Kangaroo has fewer BLUE and RED chips left, while Butterfly and Squid each hold more of the colors they personally value. The remaining gains come from fine-tuning: moving a few more RED from Kangaroo to the high-valuation players and modest BLUE transfers to whoever values it more, without leaving anyone worse off given their calculators. Strategic patience gives way to closure: players are willing to take modest but safe gains rather than hold out. Proposals in this round are more targeted and often slightly smaller in size, aiming to exploit remaining arbitrage without breaching recipients’ loss thresholds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features differentiated private values for red and blue chips, with a common value for green. All players know that green is worth $0.50 to everyone, but they do not see each other’s red/blue values; they only know their own and see trade history and inventories. The key driver of surplus is exchanging chips from players who value them less to players who value them more. Red chips are especially valuable to bat and moderately valuable to frog, but relatively weak for fox. Blue chips are most valuable to frog, then fox, then bat. Given common-value green, early green trades can serve as a safe medium of exchange or as small, low-risk tests of others’ willingness to deal. Strategic uncertainty arises from not knowing others’ exact valuations and from the random selection of partner when multiple players accept. Proposers want to craft offers that generate clear positive value for at least one counterpart while still leaving themselves a gain, and they must weigh the risk of overly generous terms (which give away surplus) against overly aggressive terms (which get rejected). Bat is numerate, patient, and exploratory, so likely to test small, slightly favorable trades and adjust as she learns. Fox is conciliatory and low-search, inclined to accept modestly beneficial deals rather than hunt for perfect ones. Frog is moderately patient and careful, likely to accept deals that show a clean gain but hesitant about small or ambiguous improvements. Over three short rounds, there is limited time to build rich inference, so players will often favor transparent, obviously win–win conversions (e.g., moving red toward bat and blue toward frog) even if those are not maximally exploitative. Randomization risk when multiple players accept also nudges proposers to design offers that are acceptable to more than one counterpart to increase execution probability, provided the expected surplus remains positive.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold symmetric inventories (10 of each color) but only know their own valuations. No public trades have occurred yet, so beliefs about others’ values are diffuse. Given her high bookkeeping and exploratory tendencies, bat is likely to start with a relatively small, low-risk test trade that uses green as a safe asset. Fox, being conciliatory and low-search, is inclined to post a straightforward, clearly advantageous swap that she believes at least one other person might take, even without precise knowledge of values. Frog, who values blue the most and is moderately patient, will likely aim to acquire blue at favorable rates and shed some red or possibly green, but will avoid trades that give away high-value blue. Overall, round 0 is about probing: small or moderately sized offers, some declined because terms are not compelling enough once private calculators are consulted.

After round 0, inventories and revealed preferences give weak but informative signals. Frog has accepted a trade where he gave up blue for green, which suggests blue is not extremely scarce to him, and bat has been willing to give up green for blue and then green for red, implying she values blue and red more than green. Fox observed these moves but still lacks precise valuations. With these cues, bat is likely to continue converting green into higher-value colors (especially red) while remaining numerically disciplined. Fox, seeing limited success on her initial attempt, may simplify her offers and aim for clearly mutual-gain exchanges, perhaps offering red (her weakest color) for blue or green. Frog now knows at least that bat is willing to trade and that green is acceptable currency; he will try to edge into more red and possibly offload some green, but will remain cautious about sacrificing too much blue unless the rate is favorable. Round 1 is thus characterized by more targeted attempts to move red toward bat and blue toward frog, but still with occasional mismatches and declined trades when calculators show thin or negative gains.

By round 2, players have clearer signals: bat has repeatedly used green as a funding source to acquire red and blue, fox has willingly moved red away and is now more concentrated in green and some blue, and frog has increased his red and blue holdings through selective trades. Everyone knows the game ends after this round, so strategic patience gives way to endgame pragmatism: each proposal is a last chance to capture surplus from any remaining misallocation. Bat, being patient but also prosocial and not highly aggressive, is likely to propose a still-profitable but not extreme offer that converts remaining green into valued colors. Fox, with low acceptance thresholds and a tendency to settle, may float a relatively simple red-for-blue or green-for-blue trade hoping for quick acceptance, though her remaining inventory limits constrain options. Frog, now holding substantial blue (which he values most) and decent red, will be reluctant to give up blue cheaply; he might propose to offload surplus green or red for small increments of blue or red, but will avoid trades that erode his gains. Some offers are still declined because they no longer offer enough incremental value given end-of-game inventories, but at least one more mutually beneficial trade is likely as players seek to tidy up positions before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip inventories (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE; GREEN is a common-value chip at $0.50 for everyone. Dog values RED at $0.50 and BLUE at $0.30, so he treats RED and GREEN as equal and BLUE as clearly inferior. Parrot and Turtle both value BLUE at $1.00, with RED worth $0.80 (Parrot) and $0.60 (Turtle). This creates a straightforward efficiency pattern: Dog should want to unload BLUE and (to a lesser degree) take BLUE from Dog is especially attractive to Parrot and Turtle, while both high-BLUE players are moderately willing to part with some RED to gain BLUE. GREEN is neutral in aggregate and serves as a convenient medium of exchange or small adjustment chip. However, each player only knows their own values. Dog sees RED and GREEN as tied and BLUE as weak; he can infer that at least one counterpart likely values some non-GREEN chip more highly, but not which or by how much. Parrot and Turtle each know that BLUE is highly valuable to themselves, but they cannot be sure whether others also prize BLUE or instead prefer RED. This uncertainty plus three-player competition for the same favorable partner creates a natural tension: everyone wants good terms, but too-aggressive surplus grabs may get rejected, and when more than one counterpart accepts, the recipient is randomized, blurring targeted bargaining.

Strategically, Dog’s conciliatory and low-aggressiveness profile suggests he will avoid extreme, one-sided proposals and will be willing to split surplus fairly when he can see he’s gaining. He is also reasonably numerate and will use the calculator to ensure non-negative value but may not fully exploit his bargaining power against misinformed partners. Parrot is also low-aggressive and somewhat prosocial, so he is inclined to accept deals that are clearly positive and not obsess about squeezing every cent, particularly if they simplify the state and reduce uncertainty. Turtle is more guarded, with a higher acceptance threshold and more concern about being exploited; she will be more selective and may reject weak or even modestly positive offers that feel lopsided or confusing, especially given her moderate numeracy and preference for clarity. Across three short rounds, everyone has limited time to learn others’ preferences from observed proposals. Most accepted trades will likely involve BLUE flowing from Dog to Parrot/Turtle in exchange for RED and/or GREEN, with terms that make all parties better off under their own valuations. Because parrot and turtle both value BLUE similarly, occasional competition for Dog’s BLUE can arise, but simultaneous responses and random recipient selection mean that competition mainly limits Dog’s ability to target his favorite partner rather than enabling him to auction off his chips. Overall, we should see a few solid, surplus-creating trades early, followed by small adjustments as players observe who seems eager for which colors.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 of each color and only knows their own chip values. Dog realizes BLUE is his weakest chip and suspects others may value it more; he is likely to start by offering BLUE for a mix of RED or GREEN on fairly balanced terms to ensure acceptance. Parrot, seeing BLUE as his best chip, will initially be reluctant to trade it away and may instead try to trade away some RED or GREEN to upgrade his mix if he can find a counterparty who appears to like RED more. Turtle, similarly high on BLUE but more guarded overall, will prefer simple offers that clearly give her more BLUE in exchange for RED or GREEN while avoiding trades that seem too generous from her perspective. Because no one has yet seen behavior from others, Round 0 is exploratory: proposals will be somewhat cautious and moderate, and acceptance behavior will help reveal who is eager to move which color.

By Round 1, the only executed trade has moved BLUE from Dog to Turtle in exchange for RED, indicating that Dog was genuinely willing to part with BLUE and that Turtle is comfortable paying with RED for BLUE. This reveals to observers that Turtle likely values BLUE more than RED and that Dog does not regard BLUE as particularly precious. Parrot saw Turtle outbid him implicitly for Dog’s BLUE (by being the one who accepted) and may infer that he faces competition for BLUE from at least one counterpart. Dog now holds slightly more RED and fewer BLUE, moving closer to his ideal of RED/GREEN-heavy holdings. Turtle, having improved her BLUE stock, may feel somewhat satisfied but will still look for clearly beneficial adjustments. Since no one has moved GREEN yet, its status as a neutral or minor balancing chip is still largely untested. Round 1 will likely feature Dog trying to continue trading away remaining BLUE, Parrot experimenting with small offers that might attract Dog or Turtle, and Turtle weighing only those proposals that clearly improve her already strong BLUE position or trade small bits of surplus at low risk.

Going into Round 2, Dog has successfully converted several BLUE chips into RED with two modestly favorable trades, confirming both his strategy of offloading BLUE and the willingness of both Parrot and Turtle to pay RED for BLUE. Parrot now has gained some BLUE from Dog but has not yet managed to reshape his holdings using his own proposals, which may slightly reduce his appetite for further risky or complex offers; his low aggressiveness encourages him to avoid pushing too hard this late. Turtle has improved her BLUE position from the first trade but declined subsequent offers that did not provide clearly better terms, consistent with her higher exploitation caution. Everyone now has some behavioral evidence: BLUE is in demand from both Parrot and Turtle, and Dog is a reliable source of BLUE who will not demand extreme compensation. In this final round, with only three turns left, players are more willing to accept small, clearly positive gains and less inclined to hold out for ideal terms. Dog may try one last minor BLUE-for-RED trade; Parrot may attempt a small adjustment involving GREEN as a balancing chip; Turtle may either try to pick up a bit more BLUE cheaply or simply pass on marginal deals that do not materially change her payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but differ sharply in private values for red and blue chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 to everyone. The real gains from trade come from moving RED and BLUE from low-value owners to high-value owners, while GREEN can serve as a neutral medium of exchange. Badger values BLUE ($0.90) slightly more than RED ($0.60); Peacock values RED very highly ($1.00) and BLUE very low ($0.10); Unicorn values BLUE very highly ($1.00) and RED low ($0.40). This creates strong two-way complementarities: Peacock wants to buy RED (especially from Badger) and sell BLUE; Unicorn wants to buy BLUE (especially from Peacock and Badger) and is relatively willing to part with RED. Badger has relatively balanced preferences but slightly favors BLUE over RED, making Badger a natural trading partner for both Peacock and Unicorn if terms are good. However, players only know their own valuations, not others', so they infer others' motives from observed proposals and acceptances. They also know that others see accurate net gains or losses on each trade for themselves, which discourages obviously exploitative offers. Persona cues suggest Peacock will be numerically precise and relatively cooperative, willing to accept modest but clear gains. Unicorn is tolerant of unequal outcomes and wants closure; he may accept moderate gains even if they seem slightly unbalanced. Badger has low search/exploration and prefers closure, so is likely to grab early clear gains rather than wait for complex multi-step improvements. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposals are broadcast to both potential partners and any over-attractive proposal risks multi-acceptance and random partner selection, forcing proposers to think about how attractive they want to make the trade for each counterparty. Over three rounds (nine total turns), the likely pattern is a few clear, positive-sum trades that move RED toward Peacock and BLUE toward Unicorn, with GREEN or relatively less-valued chips used as payment. Because there is limited time and all three are at least moderately cooperative, we expect acceptance of any clean, clearly positive-sum offer rather than long holdouts for perfect terms.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round everyone is feeling out the environment. No one yet knows others' valuations, so initial proposals serve both to seek surplus and to signal what each player might value. Badger moves first and, given low search and desire for closure, is likely to try a simple, modestly favorable trade using GREEN as neutral currency. Peacock, with strong bookkeeping, will quickly recognize where obvious gains lie (trading away BLUE and acquiring RED), but in round 0 may still be cautious about over-revealing preferences. Unicorn is relatively conciliatory and may accept moderately favorable offers to secure early gains rather than waiting for perfect information.

After round 0, Peacock has clearly revealed strong demand for RED and willingness to dump BLUE, and Unicorn has revealed strong demand for BLUE plus willingness to give up RED and some GREEN. Badger has lightly rebalanced toward BLUE and GREEN. In round 1, everyone updates: Peacock now realizes Unicorn highly values BLUE; Unicorn realizes Peacock values RED; Badger sees that both others have directional preferences. With fewer chips of their most desired colors left available, trades now focus on incremental improvements and small rebalances using GREEN and somewhat-disfavored colors as payment. Because clear gains are still available, players are inclined to keep accepting modestly favorable trades rather than hold out.

By the final round, major gains from reassigning RED and BLUE to their highest value owners have been largely exploited. Inventories are more polarized: Peacock holds more RED and fewer BLUE; Unicorn holds more BLUE and fewer RED; Badger is relatively balanced but with extra GREEN gained from earlier trades. Remaining opportunities are small, often on the margin, and may involve GREEN as fine-tuning currency. Because it is the last chance to trade, strategic patience declines: all three are somewhat more willing to accept small but strictly positive gains. However, they avoid offers that the surplus calculator shows as negative, and a few proposals may be declined simply because there is no meaningful surplus left to capture for the potential recipients.
